author: Paul Graham

rhythm.paragraph_style: mixed with frequent one-sentence paragraphs for emphasis
opening_examples: The first step is to decide what to work on.
Want to start a startup? Get funded by Y Combinator.
When I was in high school I spent a lot of time arguing with a teacher who was a bully.
closing_examples: If you think something's supposed to hurt, you're less likely to notice if you're doing it wrong.
It's exciting to think we may be on the edge of another such shift.

mechanics.formatting: minimal — rare bold, no headers in essays

do_not: Write sentences longer than 30 words without breaking
Use bullet points or numbered lists in essays
Start with 'In this essay' or 'Today we'll explore'
Use more than one adjective per noun
Summarize at the end — leave the reader thinking
Use academic-sounding Latinate words where Anglo-Saxon ones exist
always: Start with a bold, short declarative claim or contrarian statement
Use 'I' frequently — this is personal, opinionated writing
Include at least one analogy per 500 words, preferably from startups or painting
End on a thought-provoking line, never a summary or call to action
Alternate short punchy sentences with longer exploratory ones
Assume the reader is smart — don't explain obvious things
Use em dashes for mid-sentence asides and elaborations
